era: 1990s-present. Hip-Hop/DJ turntablist. E=6 -- maximum energy. Battle DJ. Stones Throw Records. Seattle-born, nationally recognized. First performing artist after radio era close.

location.formative: Seattle hip-hop scene -- battle DJ culture, crate-digging, turntablist competitions. Stones Throw Records connection bridges Seattle to national/international independent hip-hop.
location.career: DJ Revolution. Turntablist. Battle DJ. Stones Throw Records. Wake Up Show connection. National reach from Seattle origins.

members.name: DJ Revolution
members.note: Seattle-born turntablist on Stones Throw Records. Battle DJ heritage -- competition-level turntablism. Wake Up Show connection. E=6 maximum energy. First performing artist after radio era close (126-136). National reach.

associated_musicians.name: DJ Riz
associated_musicians.note: 206 Zulu. E=5. Community DJ. DJ Revolution (137, E=6) follows immediately -- community to label transition. The E=5 to E=6 step marks the performing artist return.

associated_musicians.name: DJ B-Mello
associated_musicians.note: KEXP Street Sounds. E=5. Radio DJ. Four-degree DJ sequence: B-Mello (134), Indica Jones (135), DJ Riz (136), DJ Revolution (137). Radio > community > label.

associated_musicians.name: Madlib
associated_musicians.note: Polymath producer. Madvillain (with MF DOOM). One of hip-hop's most prolific figures. Stones Throw connection places DJ Revolution in this lineage.

associated_musicians.name: MF DOOM
associated_musicians.note: The masked MC. Madvillainy. Dense wordplay, conceptual albums. Stones Throw's crown jewel. DJ Revolution's label connection includes this legacy.

associated_musicians.name: Sabzi
associated_musicians.note: Blue Scholars/Common Market producer. E=4. Seattle hip-hop production lineage. DJ Revolution extends the Seattle hip-hop thread.

theory_nodes.concept: E=6 maximum energy -- battle DJ intensity, Stones Throw label weight, post-radio-era explosion; the first E=6 in the DJ cluster; performing artists return with maximum force after eleven degrees of institutional documentation
examples: Battle turntablism: competition-speed routines compressing full vocabulary into 3-6 minutes.
Stones Throw association: Madlib, MF DOOM, J Dilla. Elite independent hip-hop company.
Post-radio explosion: E=6 after three consecutive E=5 DJs (134-136).
Wake Up Show connection: national radio platform for live turntablism.
theory_nodes.description: E=6 maximum energy. Battle DJ. Stones Throw. The energy surges.

genealogy.relationship: E=6 detonation at degree 137. Performing artists return at maximum force.

theory_nodes.concept: Stones Throw Records enters S36 -- one of the most respected independent hip-hop labels; Peanut Butter Wolf, Madlib, MF DOOM, J Dilla; vinyl-first culture, crate-digging, dusty samples; the label as cultural institution
examples: Founded 1996 by Peanut Butter Wolf. Named after Otis Redding reference.
Roster: Madlib, MF DOOM, J Dilla, Dam-Funk, Knxwledge. Hall of fame.
Aesthetic: dusty samples, vinyl warmth, handmade packaging, limited runs.
Cultural impact: defined the sound and look of independent hip-hop for decades.
theory_nodes.description: Stones Throw Records enters S36. Elite independent hip-hop label.

genealogy.relationship: Stones Throw enters at degree 137. National independent hip-hop label.

theory_nodes.concept: DJ sequence 134-137 -- four-degree progression from radio to community to label; B-Mello (134, KEXP), Indica Jones (135, KEXP), DJ Riz (136, 206 Zulu), DJ Revolution (137, Stones Throw); increasing scope and energy
examples: 134: B-Mello, KEXP hip-hop, E=5. Radio context.
135: Indica Jones, KEXP electronic, E=5. Radio context.
136: DJ Riz, 206 Zulu, E=5. Community context.
137: DJ Revolution, Stones Throw, E=6. Label context. National reach.
theory_nodes.description: Four-degree DJ sequence. Radio > community > label. E=5 > E=6.

ancestors: DJ B-Mello opened the DJ sequence at 134.
genealogy.relationship: DJ Revolution at 137 extends the DJ sequence beyond the radio era.

theory_nodes.concept: Return to performing artists after radio era (126-136) -- the S36 axis returns to performing musical artists with E=6 maximum energy; the institutional portrait is complete; the dam breaks
examples: Radio era (126-136): eleven degrees of institutional documentation.
Degree 137: first performing artist post-radio. E=6 maximum energy.
The return is explosive, not gradual. E=6 signals the dam breaking.
DJ Revolution is both the bridge (still a DJ) and the break (label artist, not radio).
theory_nodes.description: Performing artists return at degree 137. E=6 explosion.

genealogy.relationship: Performing artist return at degree 137 with maximum energy.

cross_references.relationship: DJ SEQUENCE! DJ Riz (136, E=5, 206 Zulu, community) to DJ Revolution (137, E=6, Stones Throw, label). Community to label. E=5 to E=6. The step from local to national.
DJ QUARTET! B-Mello (134), Indica Jones (135), DJ Riz (136), DJ Revolution (137). Four-degree DJ sequence. Radio > community > label. E=5, E=5, E=5, E=6.
SEATTLE HIP-HOP! Sabzi (122, E=4, producer) and DJ Revolution (137, E=6, turntablist). Different dimensions of Seattle hip-hop. Producer vs. battle DJ. Both nationally connected.
HIP-HOP LINEAGE! Nasty Nes (126, E=4, KCMU pioneer) through DJ Revolution (137, E=6, Stones Throw). The hip-hop line through S36. Radio to label.
SEATTLE HIP-HOP THREAD! Blue Scholars (99, E=5) through DJ Revolution (137, E=6). The Seattle hip-hop thread runs from conscious hip-hop to battle turntablism.
